EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 04W was intensifying into Tropical Storm Songda as it passed just south of Yap island around 1200 GST on Saturday May 21st, moving west-northwest at 6 mph. The highest sustained wind recorded at Yap Airport was from the east at 25 mph that evening at 1958 GST, and the highest gust was from the east at 49 mph at 1931 GST. The lowest sea level pressure was 997.3 mb, also at 1931 GST. Rainfall for the 24 hour period ending at 2200 GST on the 22nd was 6.11 inches. ||Only minor damage amounting to about $1,000 was sustained, mainly to crops.

At about 17:30 GST the evening of January 2nd, a six-person outrigger canoe rowed by a high-school rowing club capsized in rough water and high surf along the reef line of Tumon Bay on the west coast of Guam. A man in a kayak who went out to help was also overturned in the surf. Two of the rowers in the canoe drowned, and one was briefly hospitalized.

Four men were setting nets and fishing off Ipan Beach on the east coast of Guam between 19:00 and 21:00 GST the evening of January 22nd, when a wave washed them off the reef. Strong currents carried three of them into the open ocean while the other was able to swim to shore. A second man made it back to shore about an hour later. A search was mounted for the other two men, but several days of searching failed to locate them, and they are presumed drowned.
A 59-year-old fisherman was fishing in the shallows near the Hagatna boat Basin on the afternoon of 14 October when he was struck and killed by lightning at about 15:30 GST.

A tropical disturbance brought heavy rain and local flooding to Guam on the morning of Friday September 2nd. The highest totals were across central and northern Guam, including 5.06 inches at Guam International Airport, 5.37 inches at Andersen AFB, and 7.02 inches in Toto and at the University of Guam. Homes in low-lying areas of Yigo, Dededo, Ordot and Chalan Pago in northern and central Guam were flooded. The Red Cross housed seven familes totalling 23 people after their homes were made unsafe by the flood. No injuries were reported, and total damage is estimated at $100,000.

Terrain effects produced locally high winds on Pohnpei the afternoon of Sunday July 17th, toppling trees and utility poles, and partially or totally removing the roofs from some buildings. One man was injured when a tree fell on his car after he had arrived at home. The National Weather Service office in Kolonia reported a maximum wind gust of only 28 mph, but funneling effects produced by terrain resulted in locally damaging winds.
